prevalence.source: WHO Europe / European obstetric epidemiology syntheses
prevalence.trend: stable; risk influenced by maternal age and cardiometabolic factors

burden.mortalityRate: Low in most member states; severe morbidity persists

vulnerablePopulations: pregnant people with limited prenatal access (migrants, social vulnerability)
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Hypertension after 20 weeks with proteinuria and/or end-organ dysfunction; management per national guidelines aligned with international obstetric standards

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: prenatal BP screening is routine with high coverage in most member states
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: High in most settings due to structured prenatal care and referral pathways

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Standardized antenatal surveillance with lab testing and fetal monitoring when indicated
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Aspirin prophylaxis for high-risk pregnancies, antihypertensives, magnesium sulfate for severe features, and timely delivery planning with neonatal support
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Increasing maternal age and obesity and heterogeneity in care resources across member states
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: High prenatal care coverage, strong referral systems, and neonatal intensive care capacity in most member states
